July/August 2018

Communities: Private Practice  
Fire Protection Engineers Promote Performance-Based Design

![a builiding fire](/sites/default/files/resources/images/conference/18/PE_July_August-2018_p20.jpg)

Professional fire protection engineers are compelled to work within the boundaries of safety codes and standards—often with little leeway for innovative engineering concepts. Over the years, the Society of Fire Protection Engineers has increased efforts to help fire protection engineers and their firms incorporate more innovative performance-based design, while maximizing public safety.

According to industry experts, one of the top drivers of change in this field is performance-based design. With this type of design, practitioners must demonstrate compliance with requirements while incorporating innovative engineering.

The concept of performance-based design isn’t new, says SFPE Technical Director Chris Jelenewicz, P.E., and has been practiced throughout the history of codes and standards. Any code or standard usually has an alternative method section, he says, which allows the engineer to design while keeping the code’s intent in mind.

To promote performance-based design and address knowledge areas such as fire dynamics, fire protection systems, human behavior in fire, and risk analysis, the organization offers the SFPE Handbook of Fire Protection. The sixth edition of the reference book is in the works, but the fifth edition is currently available.

SFPE dedicates an entire conference to performance-based design to help ensure that fire protection and safety engineers can build on their knowledge base with new research, case studies, and networking opportunities. During the most recent conference in April, the sessions touched on topics including hospital fire protection; fire engineering challenges and the redevelopment of existing shopping centers; performance-based fire resilience evaluation for tall building structures; and fire safety standards and the need for global consistency. The conference also maintains an international focus as globalization increases and since performance-based design is practiced differently in countries throughout the world.

Sometimes the greatest and toughest lessons for any engineering professional can come from a tragic event. One session during the conference addressed the Marco Polo high-rise condominium fire in Honolulu, where four people died and 13 were injured last year in a building that didn’t have a proper sprinkler system. And it’s likely that the Grenfell Tower fire tragedy in London, in which 72 people perished, will also provide necessary lessons that can help save lives. “Anytime you get a catastrophe like with the Grenfell, it makes our profession stop, learn what happened, and find out ways to improve in the future,” says Jelenewicz.

#### The Workforce Outlook

![JELENEWICZ](/sites/default/files/resources/images/PEmagazine/18/PE_July_August-201821.jpg)

Fire protection engineers play an important role in ensuring that buildings and their occupants are secure in the event of a fire and can be evacuated safely. Yet, there is still a need to educate society about fire protection engineers and what they do. “Some people think we are just fire fighters, but then again, some people still don’t know what an engineer is,” says Jelenewicz.

Qualified engineers are in high demand all over this nation. But if you’re a fire protection engineer—even a recent graduate—you’re *really* in high demand. There are only a few ABET-accredited fire protection engineering and fire protection and safety technology programs in the US, says Jelenewicz. According to Glassdoor, the average annual salary for a fire protection engineer is $78,415. A senior fire and safety engineer can command a $90,000 average annual salary.

Fire protection engineering is still in its infancy compared to other engineering disciplines, says Jelenewicz. And he believes that licensure is critical because the first goal of fire protection engineering is to protect people and the second is to protect property. “The main thing that pulls people into this discipline is that there’s a strong human element behind it,” says the technical editor for *Fire Protection Engineering* magazine. “You’re helping to save people’s lives. That’s important.”

#### Core Competencies for Fire Protection Engineering

The Society of Fire Protection Engineers is in the process of recommending minimum core competencies and standards to assist jurisdictions, firms, and universities that are offering credentialing and continuing education programs for fire protection and fire safety engineers.

The SFPE Committee on Professional Qualifications has released a draft document outlining four competencies (and associated knowledge areas) that professionals should master as practicing fire protection engineers.

##### Fire Safety Science

A comprehensive understanding of the underlying physical principles of fire and its related mechanisms. This includes the principles of ignition, heat transfer, mass transfer, fire chemistry, and fire dynamics.

##### Human Behavior and Evacuation

A comprehensive understanding of human behavior and the principles of evacuation design. This includes the behavior of persons during an emergency, different design approaches, and the tools and methods to perform evacuation assessments. The knowledge areas for this competency will address physiological and psychological response, social and cultural issues, and crowd dynamics in addition to egress and life safety issues.

##### Fire Protection Systems

A comprehensive understanding of fire mitigation includes water and nonwater-based suppression, detection systems, fire modeling, fire testing, and code and standards concerns. The knowledge areas of this competency address passive systems, active systems, fire detection and alarm, and fire suppression.

##### Fire Safety Analysis

A comprehensive understanding of the principles of technical analysis related to fire safety design. This includes design approaches, concepts for evaluating design options, establishing boundary conditions, and limits of analysis and design. The knowledge areas of this competency are performance-based design, smoke management, evacuation analysis, structural fire safety, risk management, fire modeling, and fire protection-related codes and standards.

The core competencies document also addresses professional ethical conduct through the SFPE Canons of Ethics. The canons call on fire protection engineers to do the following:

*Access the “Minimum Core Competencies for Fire Protection Engineering” at [www.sfpe.org](http://www.sfpe.org).*

--- SOURCE 2: How to Improve Fire Protection Workflow Efficiencies in 2025 ---
URL: https://www.inspectpoint.com/how-to-improve-fire-protection-workflow-efficiencies/

SUMMARY:
Think of a fire protection workflow as interconnected tasks that ensure you’re implementing the most effective and efficient fire safety and business processes. Solution: Implement fire protection workflow software for real-time, on-site, electronic data capture, automated inspection, report generation, and centralized documents and evidence storage such as photos, videos, and notes. Solution: Use fire inspection software with automated maintenance reminders, scheduling tools, and other automated functions to ensure you’re always working proactively, taking preventive measures, reducing equipment malfunctions and downtime, and enhancing fire safety activities. By embracing data analytics and eliminating much of the guesswork that goes along with manual, outdated processes, fire protection teams can harness computer-powered insights (like machine learning and artificial intelligence) to optimize workflows, inspection findings, maintenance records, and equipment performance metrics.

FULL CONTENT:
How to Improve Fire Protection Workflow Efficiencies

by Inspect Point Team | Mar 15, 2024

A Burning Need for Fire Protection Workflow Improvements

In an industry where fire and life safety co-exist in a delicate balance, fire protection is not just a responsibility; it’s a critical lifeline for people and property protection. Yet, inefficient fire protection systems and poorly planned schedules and workflows abound. Not only does this slow processes like inspections and repairs, but a simple oversight or malfunction caused by inefficient workflows can quickly escalate into a devastating inferno, putting lives at risk and damaging property and livelihoods.

As a whole, the industry has been slow to adopt innovative technologies. As a result, fire professionals struggle with automating and standardizing critical workflows — which could ultimately be the difference between chaos and control, minimizing risks and ensuring swift, effective responses.

The reality is the cost of fire protection inefficiency can be measured in more than just dollars and cents. Delayed inspections, missed maintenance deadlines, and siloed, proprietary data can create a flashpoint for hazards, potentially leading to costly property damage, business disruptions, and even loss of life. Additionally, non-compliance with regulations can result in hefty fines and legal penalties, further impacting operational resilience, financial stability, and business reputation.

When you consider the benefits of optimizing fire protection workflows that extend far beyond mitigating adverse outcomes, it’s hard to see why many fire industry professionals are hesitant to adopt new software and tools that could make their jobs so much easier — and decrease the potential for errors or oversights.

With the right tools, manual inspection tracking could become a thing of the past where, instead, inspectors can effortlessly conduct meticulous and efficient reviews and access relevant data quickly for more informed decision-making. Automation technologies can facilitate everything from proactively addressing maintenance tasks to minimizing equipment downtime and eliminating potential fire hazards and safety risks.

Most importantly, enhancing fire protection workflows, regardless of your role in the industry, can provide peace of mind and confidence in knowing your fire protection workflows are operating at optimal levels.

Investing in efficient fire safety workflows is not just a sound business decision; it’s imperative. By streamlining processes, leveraging technology, and embracing data-driven insights, you can proactively transform operations to safeguard lives and property while ensuring your business’ success and sustainability.

What is a Fire Protection Workflow?

A fire protection workflow is different by company and role, but typically involves a work process from scheduling to payment collection. Think of a fire protection workflow as interconnected tasks that ensure you’re implementing the most effective and efficient fire safety and business processes. It’s a strategic roadmap and should include everything you need to implement, monitor, manage, and maintain fire safety strategies and operations. It encompasses everything from inspections and maintenance to data analytics and reporting, aiming to prevent fires proactively, minimize damage, and improve job performance — with less lift and fewer manual processes — all while ensuring compliance and business continuity.

Fire Protection Workflow Examples

Streamlined processes and innovative fire workflow solutions empower fire professionals and their teams with actionable outcomes that manual tasks can’t. By strategically integrating technology and proven practices into your workflows, you can boost productivity and, ultimately, enhance safety.

Here are some examples of fire protection workflows across various roles in the industry:

Fire Protection Business Owner or Manager

Fire Protection Inspector

Fire Protection Technician

Fire Protection Engineer

Common Fire Protection Workflow Challenges

For fire protection, meticulousness and efficiency are paramount. Yet, many professionals still use complicated spreadsheets or manual paper tracking that hinder their ability to do their jobs as effectively as possible with little confidence they’re not overlooking safety standards or customer requirements.
Here are some common inefficiencies often found in fire protection workflows:

Paper-Based Systems and Manual Data Entry

Mountains of paper forms, handwritten inspection reports and work orders, and scattered spreadsheets with disparate data create data management nightmares. Employees waste time collecting and processing data, with some information requiring manual inputs multiple times across multiple systems, which organically increases human error, creates blind spots in workflows, and frustrates everyone.

Solution: Implement fire protection workflow software for real-time, on-site, electronic data capture, automated inspection, report generation, and centralized documents and evidence storage such as photos, videos, and notes.

Lack of Communication and Collaboration

Fire safety involves multiple stakeholders, from the front office to inspectors and technicians to engineers and business owners — and don’t forget your customers, too. However, siloed communication often leads to misunderstandings, delays, and missed deadlines.

Solution: Foster transparency and collaboration through integrated communication tools within a fire protection solution. Utilize real-time updates, get instant feedback, and share project management information to keep everyone on the same page.

Siloed Information and Data Fragmentation

Valuable data often gets trapped in separate documents, spreadsheets, and unconnected databases. Fragmentation makes it challenging to identify trends, analyze performance, and make data-driven decisions.

Solution: Implement fire safety software with centralized data storage, document and evidence repositories, and integrated reporting. Routine review of data across all parts of the business to get insights into areas for improvement, resource allocation, and overall process effectiveness.

Proactive Practices

The industry has long been reactive to issues such as repairs. Unfortunately, that creates unnecessary downtime and potentially compromises safety. Scheduling and tracking preventive maintenance tasks is cumbersome and prone to oversights.

Solution: Use fire inspection software with automated maintenance reminders, scheduling tools, and other automated functions to ensure you’re always working proactively, taking preventive measures, reducing equipment malfunctions and downtime, and enhancing fire safety activities.

Lack of Resources

Budget constraints and limited personnel are common issues in the industry that often strain fire protection teams, making keeping up with inspections, maintenance, and other demands challenging.

Solution: Streamline workflows with technology. Automating administrative tasks frees staff time and resources, allowing them to focus on other activities and strategic initiatives.

By tackling these common challenges, fire protection teams can achieve smoother workflows and better collaboration, ultimately creating a safer environment for everyone.

Strategies for Fire Protection Workflow Efficiency

Whether you’re a fire protection business owner, a fire protection technician, engineer, or inspector, adopting workflow strategies can improve efficiency and save time and money. Optimizing fire safety workflows can also enhance safety, which is a win for everyone involved in protecting life and property from fire and fire hazards.

Some benefits of adopting workflow optimization strategies:

While you’ll best understand your unique business needs, here are some best practice workflow strategies to consider:

Adopt Industry-Recognized Fire Protection Software

Fire inspection software streamlines processes, replacing paper forms with electronic workflows, data gathering, and reports. With software, you can capture data on-site in real-time, eliminating manual entry errors and saving valuable time. Look for a solution that enables photo and video capture directly within the software so your teams can build a comprehensive digital record of each inspection. Additionally, seek a solution that will automate scheduling with system-wide updates whenever there are changes, send task reminders, and also has work order management tools.

Enhancing fire protection workflows, regardless of your role in the industry, can provide peace of mind and confidence in knowing your fire protection workflows are operating at optimal levels.

Cloud-based fire inspection software offers centralized data storage and accessibility anytime, from wherever you are — whether that’s someone in the office managing workflows across departments or team members who need to access inspection reports, maintenance records, and building plans from any device. Cloud-based solutions are a valuable resource that fosters collaboration and facilitates informed decision-making.

Implement Workflow Automation

Embracing digital solutions streamlines workflows and enhances efficiency, but technology’s value is more than just digitization. Automating repetitive, manual tasks frees employee time for other, more crucial activities. Standardized operating procedures, checklists, and templates further improve efficiency and accuracy. For example, technicians following predefined steps for inspections and maintenance minimize errors and increase consistency across your business, regardless of individual experience. It also helps to improve speed and reduce risks associated with human error.

Develop Employee Fire Safety Training

Technology empowers, but doesn’t replace experience and expertise. Routine employee fire safety training is essential. Develop strategies for your employees or team that support ongoing education and refresher courses to ensure knowledge retention. Plan for specialized training so your staff has needed skills for specific tasks or equipment. Build a fire safety culture within your organization.

Conduct Fire Protection Audits

Fire protection audits are the cornerstone of continuous improvement. With an audit, mainly when supported by software and data analytics, you can identify inefficiencies, uncover potential safety hazards, and highlight areas for optimization. Automatic report generation helps improve inspection findings over time, reveals trends, and pinpoints areas requiring more attention or updated workflows.

Partner with Industry Experts and Consultants

Partnering with industry experts and consultants is invaluable, especially when attracting and hiring skilled fire protection specialists is challenging. By working with industry experts, you can access support for things like complex fire hazard assessments, intricate system installations, or code interpretations without hiring more staff. Subject matter experts paired with your team’s knowledge and technology-enhanced workflows ensure optimal protection for lives and property.

Make Data-Driven Business Decisions

By embracing data analytics and eliminating much of the guesswork that goes along with manual, outdated processes, fire protection teams can harness computer-powered insights (like machine learning and artificial intelligence) to optimize workflows, inspection findings, maintenance records, and equipment performance metrics. By routinely evaluating data points, you can find inefficiencies and take proactive measures to resolve them before a real-life issue occurs. Data analytics can help you navigate more efficient resource allocation, target training, and develop proactive safety measures.

Tips and Best Practices for Fire Protection Workflows

You’re not alone if you are overwhelmed by fire protection paperwork, inspections, and endless checklists. Whether you’re a fire inspector scrambling through piles of reports, a technician drowning in data entry, or a business owner juggling compliance requirements, streamlining your fire protection workflows is challenging.
Here are some best practices to help transform your fire safety work processes with efficiency and automation:

Fire Protection Workflow Efficiency Tips for Owners

Implement field service management software Automate tasks like scheduling appointments, dispatching technicians, generating work orders, collecting data on-site, and invoicing customers. Eliminating manual processes saves time, reduces errors, and ensures consistency in service delivery.

Look for features like:

Automate data collection and reporting

Paper forms and spreadsheets slow processes and are prone to errors. Utilize electronic forms, templates, and mobile apps to electronically capture inspection data, service reports, and other crucial information. Integrate these forms with your field service software for real-time data entry, insight, and report generation.

Standardize inspection and service procedures

Develop clear, concise checklists and protocols for inspections, work order completion, installations, and maintenance and fire safety procedures to ensure technicians perform tasks consistently and efficiently. This can help decrease process variability from one employee to another so you can ensure your team delivers an optimal and approved experience with less chance of error. Make these procedures available through mobile apps or other software so your technicians and inspectors can access them quickly in the field.

Leverage communication tools

Implement fire protection software to facilitate seamless communication between technicians, inspectors, engineers, office staff, and customers. This allows for real-time updates, faster issue resolution, and improved collaboration.

Fire protection workflow efficiency tips for technicians

Embrace mobile technologies

Ditch paper forms and use digital inspection apps on your tablet, phone, laptop, or computer to streamline data collection, pre-populate standard fields, and instantly capture photos, videos, and notes. Use a fire inspection solution that enables work order creation in the field, scheduling and updates, and provides client information directly to you wherever you are. Your solution should update in real-time, eliminating the need for manual reports or having to go back or call into the office. You can also use mobile apps to quickly access standardized procedures, compliance and regulatory requirements, maintenance manuals, and safety protocols so you don’t have to carry around binders or books, and you can let your office staff focus on other tasks instead of looking up information for you.

Use automated communication tools

Automated communication tools with reminders for schedules, inspections, equipment testing, maintenance deadlines, and other tasks can ensure you never miss a critical assignment or deadline and are always focused on compliance and professional standards. Fire protection workflow software that enables communication with colleagues, inspectors, and customers helps you resolve issues faster, share updates quickly, and improve collaboration. You can also benefit from using software that automatically generates inspection reports and service documentation based on your data inputs so you can save time on paperwork and focus on critical tasks.

Standardize your workflows

A company with standardized procedures can ensure a consistent, quality customer experience regardless of which employee responds to a need. Technicians can benefit from software that facilitates standardized inspection, installation, and maintenance procedures. Consistency ensures quality and reduces errors. By using digital checklists and templates for inspections and service reports, you can avoid missing crucial steps and provide thorough documentation while always adhering to your established company protocols for communication, data entry, and safety best practices.

Fire protection workflow efficiency tips for fire inspectors

Embrace inspection software

Digital checklists and reports let you ditch the mountain of paper forms that keep you buried under manual, repetitive tasks. Instead, electronic inspection software can provide pre-populated checklists and templates to ensure you cover all essential points. Auto-generated reports and work orders save time on documentation. And, with integrated photos, videos, and note storage, you’ll no longer need multiple devices or have to go into the office to complete evidence collection. Bonus points if the solution offers offline functionalities so you can work seamlessly even without an internet connection. Capture data on-site and synchronize it later, maximizing your time in the field.

Leverage communication and collaboration tools

Instantly share inspection data and reports with colleagues, supervisors, and building owners to increase transparency and facilitate action items for deficiencies and other critical findings. Other tools, like video conferencing, can help you conduct virtual walkthroughs, consultations, and meetings while reducing travel time and expenses. You can also benefit from using a solution with automated notifications and reminders so you know when and where your next appointment is and can easily follow up on inspections, code updates, and other issues to increase compliance and accountability.

Standardize inspection practices

Standardized inspection protocols for different building types, occupancy classifications, and code requirements can facilitate consistent service, thoroughness, and fair evaluations. By using automated, standardized checklists, inspection guides, and reference materials, you’ll always have access to the resources you need to ensure a consistent application. Standardized data entry procedures can also provide consistent reporting and practical data analysis.

Stay up-to-date with technology and regulations

Explore industry-recognized technologies to simplify day-to-day tasks. Also, consider taking advantage of training opportunities, continuous education, code updates, and best practices to stay ahead of the curve and ensure accurate assessments that meet current requirements.

Software for Fire Protection Workflows

As a fire safety professional, your days are likely filled with inspections, reports, deadlines, and more paperwork than anyone wants to deal with. Although fire professionals are great at reducing the chance of fire infernos, they’re often awful at fighting internal fires of inefficiency, especially if you’re still using dated, manual processes for your daily tasks. Instead, consider all the benefits of using fire protection workflow software specifically designed to empower you and extinguish your workflow headaches.

With the right fire safety software, you can:

Meet Compliance Requirements For Fire Protection Systems

For many fire safety professionals, navigating the evolving compliance landscape is a constant source of stress and inefficiency. But the good news is, just like your other workflows, you can streamline and automate many of your compliance tasks while always using the most up-to-date standards, fire codes, and regulations.

With a solution like Inspect Point, you can get instant access to relevant codes and regulations within the software, ensuring compliance consistency, accuracy, and confidence. You can also generate automated compliance reports with pre-filled data, saving you hours of manual work and ensuring error-free record-keeping.

Inspect Point is also a great tool to help you overcome many of the common pain points you work through every day, such as:

Other ways Inspect Point makes compliance a breeze:

--- SOURCE 3: [PDF] Innovative Fire Protection ---
URL: https://www.nist.gov/system/files/documents/el/fire_protection/04FireProtection_web.pdf

SUMMARY:
Lack of a quantitative understanding of uncertainty and system performance hinders the development of rational safety factors in fire protection engineer-.

FULL CONTENT:
What is the problem? The cost of fire in the U.S. is growing. In 2007, direct property loss due to fire was $14.6 billion and the total burden of fire on the U.S. economy is estimated to be around $320 billion/year. In 2006, the annual losses attributable to fire included 3,430 civilian lives and 17,675 serious injuries. Fire service losses included about 103 lives and about 83,000 injuries. Fire losses from systemic causes are preventable. Significant damage from wildland-urban interface (WUI) fires is on the rise in the U.S. There have been three major WUI fire loss events in the last six years: The 2003 Cedar fire in California cost $2 billion in insured losses and destroyed 3,600 homes, the October 2007 southern California fires which displaced residents of over 300,000 homes, and the 2009 Station fire. Overall, the trends suggest that the severity of the U.S. fire problem is growing.
There is an incomplete understanding of fire behavior, which hinders the develop-ment of innovative fire protection. Current prescriptive fire standards and codes stifle innovation in fire safety systems, tech-nologies, and building design. To ensure fire safety in a cost-effective manner and to reduce fire losses, it is essential that adequate science-based tools are developed to enable the implementation of the next Innovative Fire Protection generation of standards, codes, and tech-nologies that address the U.S. fire problem. Measurement science is lacking to reduce the risk of fire spread in buildings, to reduce fire spread in WUI communities, to ensure effective and safe use of emerging fire ser-vice technologies, and to derive lessons from fire investigations.
Addressing these measurement science needs is essential if fire losses are to be reduced and the resilience of buildings and communities (people and property) are to be increased.
Why is it hard to solve? Fire is a complex set of dynamic interacting processes that are inadequately under-stood. Unwanted fires occur in unconfined geometries with heterogeneous mixes of fuel, uncontrolled air supply and uncertain ignition events. Fires are characterized by a multiplicity of time and length scales, and the complexity of the phenomena that con-trol fire dynamics renders computer predic-tions approximate, at best. While soot is a dominant factor in the radiative heat transfer that controls the rate of fire spread and the formation of deadly smoke, key mechanisms that dictate the amount of soot are not yet tractable. Quantitative measurements at full-scale can be dangerous, limited by environmental constraints, technically Measurement Science for BUILDING & FIRE RESEARCH LABORATORY M e a s u r e m e n t S c i e n c e f o r innovative fire protection M e a s u r e m e n t S c i e n c e f o r innovative fire protection challenging, and expensive. Solutions to reducing losses extend beyond fire dynamics to include structural dynam-ics and human behavior within a fire environment. Manufacturers of new fire technologies lack the measurement science to evaluate the performance of products due to complexities of fire dynamics and man-machine interac-tions in a fire environment.
Why BFRL? NIST has sole statutory responsibility under the Fire Prevention and Control Act of 1974 to support fire safety research for constructed facilities. Research on this strategic goal is sup-ported by the core competency of the Building and Fire Research Laboratory (BFRL) in fire protection and fire spread within buildings and communities. BFRL has strong links with industry, the fire services, the construction communities, and codes and standards organizations, which enable rapid dissemination of new knowledge, tools and measure-ment science results from laboratory to practice. BFRL staff has leadership posi-tions on key U.S. and international stand-ards committees that will make use of the research results. This strategic goal supports recommendations from nationally recognized reports, studies and investigations.
Contact: dr. Anthony Hamins anthony.hamins@nist.gov Reduced Risk of Fire Hazard in Buildings The lack of measurement science (including knowledge, standard test methods, methodologies to interpret the test results, and validated predictive methods) form a substantial technical barrier to the development and introduc-tion of innovative fire protection involving materials, building design, and technol-ogy. The current gaps in understanding (including modeling capability, appropri-ate test methods, and methodologies) preclude the ability to demonstrate that proposed innovations are technically feasible, safe, and economically viable. Lack of a quantitative understanding of uncertainty and system performance hinders the development of rational safety factors in fire protection engineer-ing analysis and design.
The objective of this program is to reduce the risk of fire spread in buildings through the development and implemen-tation of measurement science. BFRL is developing and quantifying the accuracy of performance-based design tools and analysis, and creating the scientific basis for the next generation of standards, codes, and fire measurement techniques.
Performance-based design of build-ings requires validated tools to justify equivalent safety when compared to prescriptive code requirements. Without the necessary tools to develop and quan-tify the cost of possible design options, architects and engineers are constrained from realizing effective solutions. A performance-based option has been included in the National Fire Protection Association’s Life Safety Code, and the Society of Fire Protection Engineers has published engineering design guides to facilitate implementation and best prac-tices in the use of performance-based fire protection techniques. The program includes several major measurement science research thrusts, each with corresponding new techni-cal ideas. The emphasis of the work is to ensure that scientific knowledge can be effectively disseminated to facilitate standards and code development and to enhance the capabilities and the scope of the predictive models, which are associ-ated with all four thrust areas. The new technical ideas are described below for each of the major thrust areas.
Predictive Fire Model Development: Three areas are emphasized: algorithm development, verification and validation, Calculation using NIST's Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS) software to simulate a fire burning a liquid hydrocarbon fuel. A blow-up of one section of the flowfield highlights computational techniques being developed to enable more accurate calculations.
BFRL activities, accomplishments & recognitions and user support. The new idea is to improve and expand the predictive capability of current fire models using sub-models that better simulate criti-cal physical and chemical processes in fires. New data generated on solid and gas-phase phenomena is guiding sub-model development and validation of material burning and soot emission. A Configuration Management Plan is being established that automates maintenance with full revision tracking documentation and coordinates software development within an established set of guidelines. Advanced Fire Measurements: The new idea is to develop new measure-ment techniques that are well-char-acterized and accurate, that improve our understanding of fire physics, and facilitate innovation in fire protection. The area is also providing critical data to guide the development and enable vali-dation of predictive models. Research is focused on development of innovative measurement techniques (e.g., real-time extractive soot measurements), improvement of traditional measure-ment techniques (including heat release rate and bi-directional velocity probe), and using these techniques to improve our understanding of the structure of compartment fires. These results are being used to guide and validate the NIST Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS) software, a predictive fire model.
Fire Protection in Buildings: New theory is being developed and new data is being acquired to characterize performance in critical areas of fire protection in build-ing systems. The research includes the development of a decision support software tool for communities interested in cost-effective investment in water sprinklers, an egress model based on data taken during fire drill evacuations from select building types, and full-scale experiments that examine the effects of detector type, placement, and fuel source on the performance of smoke detectors. Hydrogen Fire Safety: Research is being conducted to develop emerging technol-ogies in detection and predictive meth-ods to hydrogen safety. Experiments are being used to assess the effectiveness of the Fire Dynamics Simulator (FDS) software for predicting the dispersion behavior of a buoyant gas and to identify necessary modifications to the model. Hydrogen gas sensor performance is being tested in the BFRL Hydrogen Detector Environment Evaluator facility.
BFRL continues to work with industry, fire testing laboratories, national and international organizations that deter-mine fire standards and codes, university researchers, other government agen-cies, and international fire researchers to reduce fire losses. This program is pro-viding the knowledge and tools to reduce systemic fire losses as the recognized source of accurate measurement and prediction methods used in practice.
Contact: dr. Anthony Hamins anthony.hamins@nist.gov Reduced Risk of Fire Spread in Wildland-Urban Interface Communities Wildland-urban interface (WUI) fires occur when wildland fires cannot be con-trolled, often due to extreme wind and fuel conditions, and spread into com-munities. These fires occur worldwide. In the last ten years, significant dam-age from WUI fires has occurred in the U.S., Australia, and the Mediterranean region. Even with the increased level of risk reduction effort through wildland-fuel treatments, California has suffered three major WUI fire loss events in the last six years. Since little experimental or post-WUI fire analysis has been con-ducted to evaluate existing risk mitigation approaches for a representative range of WUI environments, their effectiveness is generally unproven (from both a risk reduction and an economic point of view).
Destructive WUI fires often occur over large scales and in extreme BFRL conducts experi-ments of burning trees in order to validate pre-dictions of heat fluxes and heat release from simulated trees using the Wildland-Urban Interface Fire Dynamics Simulator (WFDS). WFDS is a physics based numerical modeling approach which includes all modes of heat trans-fer (convection, conduc-tion, and radiation).
M e a s u r e m e n t S c i e n c e f o r innovative fire protection BFRL 100 Bureau Drive Gaithersburg, MD 20899-8600 301.975.5900 http://www.bfrl.nist.gov environmental conditions that are diffi-cult to reproduce experimentally or mea-sure with field deployable instrumenta-tion. This presents significant challenges to effective modeling, experimental, and field based research. The overall objective of this program is to evaluate and improve the tools used by communities, homeowners, and fire officials to effectively and economically reduce damages and improve safety in WUI fires. BFRL is developing improved tools via advances in measurement science through collaborative and syn-ergistic projects involving laboratory and field measurement, post-fire analysis, physics-based fire behavior modeling, economic cost analysis models, and workshops with stakeholders. This program will lead to reduction of costs due to WUI fires by providing the measurement science to develop and evaluate new standard test methods, building codes, and structure retrofitting techniques. Similarly, the adoption of improved and tested WUI community-scale risk assessment and mitigation methodologies will lead to reduced damage and improved emergency responder safety. Stakeholders include homeowners, homeowner associations, city fire departments, federal and state fire officials, building material manufacturers, homebuilders, standards organizations, and fire researchers.
Contact: dr. William Mell ruddy@nist.gov Advanced Fire Service Technologies At an increasing rate, the fire service is learning to put to use existing tech-nologies such as thermal imaging and positive pressure ventilation techniques, and is anticipating the integration of new innovative technologies, such as tactical decision aids, training simulators, and improved protective clothing. For existing technologies, it is critical that perfor-mance be measured and evaluated in a scientifically sound manner and that the technology be successfully transferred to the fire service through training pro-grams and fire fighting simulators. For emerging technology, industry needs science-based performance metrics to evaluate and improve their products and develop new technology, and an under-standing of the requirements of the fire service. For both existing and emerging technologies, it is critical that the tech-nology be successfully transferred to the fire service through computer model simulations, virtual training programs and science-based training materials. BFRL is taking a leadership role that is recognized nationally and internationally for the transfer of science, metrics, and technology, into the hands of fire fighters, incident commanders, and other first responders. Approximately half of the measurement science effort is focused on providing the science that supports the development of performance metrics, standards, and testing protocols for existing equipment and technology. About forty percent of the measurement science effort is applied to emerging innovative technologies that allow the fire service to quickly take advantage of the technology and/or the information rich environment. The remaining ten percent of the measurement science effort is directed at fire fighting simula-tors and training programs to insure that the above science and technology can be successfully transferred in a usable form to the fire service.
Through this program, fire fighting technology developers, manufacturers, and users will have a proven set of per-formance metrics, standards, and testing protocols for evaluating existing tech-nology and developing new technology. The results of the program will provide uniform, unbiased, and science-based standards that will encourage improv-ing current fire service equipment and enable the creation of next generation fire service technologies.
Contact: mr. Nelson Bryner nelson.bryner@nist.gov BFRL fire protection engineers use an abandoned New York City brick high-rise as a seven-story fire laboratory to better understand the fast-moving spread of wind-driven flames, smoke and toxic gases through corridors and stairways of burning buildings.

# Structural Fire Engineering for Innovative Solutions

by Mikko Salminen

New and rapidly changing building trends have resulted in designers increasingly searching for alternatives to conventional structural fire protection approaches. Structural fire engineering (SFE) offers an innovative, cost-effective way to optimize fire protection while reducing dependence on conventional measures.

Mikko Salminen, PhD, discusses the value of structural fire engineering in his article for *Consulting-Specifying Engineer.* A practical method for determining the potential impact of a fire on a building’s structural elements, SFE helps ensure that structural members and systems within buildings, whether steel, timber or concrete, can maintain structural integrity during a fire.

Prescriptive building codes and standards are often unable to account for all possible cases and solutions for structural fire protection. Structural fire engineering may be the only way for a building to demonstrate a sufficient level of safety and address the fire safety challenges related to new building products and designs.

Incorporating SFE from the early design stages can produce innovative, sustainable and aesthetically pleasing buildings while ensuring compliance with fire safety standards and potentially reducing construction and maintenance costs.

Read the article [Structural Fire Engineering for Innovative Solutions](https://www.csemag.com/articles/structural-fire-engineering-for-innovative-solutions/) in *Consulting-Specifying Engineer.*

enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovations in fire safety engineering, such as performance-based design, enhance compliance with safety standards and improve overall fire protection strategies, ultimately safeguarding lives and property.

Advancements like automated maintenance reminders and integrated inspection software streamline fire protection workflows, significantly reducing human error and enhancing the safety of buildings and their occupants.

Innovative fire protection measures prioritize sustainability, reducing reliance on conventional materials and methods, thereby minimizing ecological footprints while maintaining effective safety standards.
